question: How does the NuScale passive safety approach compare to active safety systems in conventional PWRs?

nuscale_ch01_0020.title: Safety Considerations
nuscale_ch01_0020.content:
NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report General Plant Description
Tier 2 1.2-7 Revision 5The CNV is maintained at a vacuum un der normal operating conditions. The 
benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the CNV include:
• minimizes moisture content that could im pact the reliability and contribute to 
corrosion of components within the CNV
• facilitates detection of leakage from the reactor coolant pressure boundary
• eliminates convective heat transfer and therefore, the need for RPV insulation, 
which reduces potential debr is generated in the CNV
• limits the initial amount of oxygen  in containment (severe accident 
combustible gas consideration)
Following an actuation of the ECCS, stea m is vented from the RPV through the 
reactor vent valves. This results in an initial spike in containment pressure and 
temperature. Steam in contact with the inside surface of the CNV is passively 
cooled and condensed by conduction and convection to the reactor pool water. 
This passive process rapidly reduces co ntainment pressure and temperature and 
maintains containment pressure and temper ature at less than design conditions 
indefinitely.
1.2.1.1.3 Safety Considerations
NuScale has achieved an improvement in safety over existing plants through 
simplicity of design, reliance on passive safety systems, and small fuel inventory. 
The integral design of the NPM eliminat es external coolant loop piping, which 
eliminates large-break LOCA scenarios. The availability of passive safety systems for 
decay heat removal, emergency core co oling, and control room habitability 
eliminates the need for external powe r under accident conditions. With these 
passive safety systems, small-break LOCAs do  not challenge the safety of the plant. 
The result is a design with a core damage  frequency that is lower than the current 
light water reactor fleet.
The reactor core has a small radioactive so urce term as compared to a conventional 
1,000 MWe nuclear reactor. Based on the sm aller fuel inventory, the amount of 
radioactive material available for release d uring a postulated accident is reduced. 
Table 1.2-2 provides a listing of some of the features of the NPM.
1.2.1.2 Engineered Safety F eatures and Emergency Systems
1.2.1.2.1 Engineered Safety Feature Materials
Details are provided in Sect ion 6.1 related to the select ion and fabrication methods 
for metallic and organic materials used in ESF components to ensure compatibility 
with fluids that the component may be  exposed to during normal, accident, 
maintenance, and testing conditions.
1.2.1.2.2 Containment Systems
The containment is an integr al part of the NPM and pr ovides primary containment 
for the RCS. Section 6.2 provides furthe r information for the containment system.

--- Referenced Tables (structured) ---
[Table 1.2-2: Design Features of a NuScale Power Module]
RCS contained within the RPV | No large diameter primary coolant
piping | Eliminates postulated large-break LOCA
spectrum accidents
Natural-convection-cooled core | No reactor coolant pumps | Eliminates reactor coolant pump accidents,
shaft breaks, pump seizure, missile generation
and pump leaks
High containment design pressure | Containment peak pressure for
worst case design-basis accident
remains below containment design
pressure | Containment integrity assured, minimizing the
potential for radioactive releases during
postulated accidents.
RPV and NSSS | During an accident, any water lost
from RPV stays within containment
and is returned to the RPV by
passive means | No postulated design-basis small-break LOCA
capable of uncovering nuclear fuel
Evacuated containment | Subatmospheric pressure during
normal operation
No insulation on RPV | Minimal amount of noncondensible gases
increases the steam condensation rate for
containment heat removal during postulated
small-break LOCA. Amount of oxygen in
containment during normal operations is
minimized.
Eliminates potential sump screen blockage and
permits cooling of the exterior of the vessel
during an accident
Low power core (160 MWt) | Reduces decay heat removal
requirements | Enhances in-vessel retention; maintains low
accident consequences; reduces fission
product source term; simplifies emergency
planning
Reactor pool with partially
(approximately 90%) immersed NPM | CNV | Provides passive long-term cooling
Passive safety systems | Safety systems cool and
depressurize the RPV/CNV even in
the event of loss of external power | Active safety systems are not required
nuscale_ch01_0020.summary: The NuScale design enhances safety by maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), which reduces corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits combustible gases. Its passive safety systems and integral design eliminate the need for external power and large coolant loops, resulting in a lower core damage frequency and a smaller radioactive source term compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0829.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with SRP 17.5, Rev 1 requirements for quality assurance in design certification, early site permits, and new COL applicants, specifically regarding procurement document control and related criteria. Most criteria are fully met, but some—such as document control, inspection, and activities like identification and control of materials—are only partially applicable or are the responsibility of the COL applicant referencing the certified design. The table clarifies which requirements are met, partially met, or not applicable, aligning with NUREG-0800 guidance.

nuscale_ch01_0052.title: Comparison with Other Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0052.content:
NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report Comparison with Other Facilities
Tier 2 1.3-1 Revision 51.3 Comparison with Other Facilities
The major NuScale Power Plant design feature s and nominal parameters are provided in 
Table 1.3-1 and discussed further in the associated final safety anal ysis report (FSAR) section(s). 
These NuScale features and values  are shown in comparison with a typical pressurized water 
reactor (PWR) plant design. All values are no minal and provided for comparison only. The 
typical PWR values presented are representative of the Standardized Nu clear Unit Power Plant 
System design.
Table 1.3-2 provides a comparison of safety sy stems and components re quired to protect the 
reactor core for the NuScale Power Plant versus a typical PWR plant.NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report Comparison with Other Facilities

nuscale_ch01_0052.summary: This section compares the major design features and parameters of the NuScale Power Plant with those of a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR). It provides a side-by-side summary of key characteristics such as electrical output, core design, reactor coolant system, and safety components, highlighting the differences in scale, configuration, and safety system requirements between NuScale and conventional PWRs. The comparison is intended for reference and uses nominal values.

nuscale_ch01_0022.title: Engineered Safety Feature Materials
nuscale_ch01_0022.content:
NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report General Plant Description
Tier 2 1.2-7 Revision 5The CNV is maintained at a vacuum un der normal operating conditions. The 
benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the CNV include:
• minimizes moisture content that could im pact the reliability and contribute to 
corrosion of components within the CNV
• facilitates detection of leakage from the reactor coolant pressure boundary
• eliminates convective heat transfer and therefore, the need for RPV insulation, 
which reduces potential debr is generated in the CNV
• limits the initial amount of oxygen  in containment (severe accident 
combustible gas consideration)
Following an actuation of the ECCS, stea m is vented from the RPV through the 
reactor vent valves. This results in an initial spike in containment pressure and 
temperature. Steam in contact with the inside surface of the CNV is passively 
cooled and condensed by conduction and convection to the reactor pool water. 
This passive process rapidly reduces co ntainment pressure and temperature and 
maintains containment pressure and temper ature at less than design conditions 
indefinitely.
1.2.1.1.3 Safety Considerations
NuScale has achieved an improvement in safety over existing plants through

The integral design of the NPM eliminat es external coolant loop piping, which 
eliminates large-break LOCA scenarios. The availability of passive safety systems for

The result is a design with a core damage  frequency that is lower than the current 
light water reactor fleet.
The reactor core has a small radioactive so urce term as compared to a conventional 
1,000 MWe nuclear reactor. Based on the sm aller fuel inventory, the amount of 
radioactive material available for release d uring a postulated accident is reduced. 
Table 1.2-2 provides a listing of some of the features of the NPM.
1.2.1.2 Engineered Safety F eatures and Emergency Systems
1.2.1.2.1 Engineered Safety Feature Materials
Details are provided in Sect ion 6.1 related to the select ion and fabrication methods 
for metallic and organic materials used in ESF components to ensure compatibility 
with fluids that the component may be  exposed to during normal, accident,

1.2.1.2.2 Containment Systems
The containment is an integr al part of the NPM and pr ovides primary containment 
for the RCS. Section 6.2 provides furthe r information for the containment system.

nuscale_ch01_0022.summary: The NuScale reactor’s containment vessel (CNV) is maintained under vacuum during normal operation, which reduces corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits combustible gases. Its passive safety features—including elimination of large external coolant loops and reliance on passive cooling and emergency systems—result in improved safety, lower core damage frequency, and a smaller radioactive source term compared to conventional reactors.

It confirms that NuScale meets specific criteria for system pressure, critical heat flux, event classification, and instrument setpoints, aligning with NRC guidance and ensuring plant safety under these conditions. The report references compliance with relevant standards, including RG 1.105 and the Standard Review Plan.

nuscale_ch01_0063.title: Emergency Core Cooling System Valve Design Certification Application Demonstration Test
nuscale_ch01_0063.content:
NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report Requirements for Additional Technical Information
Tier 2 1.5-7 Revision 5Test results confirmed the operability of th e control rod drive sh afts for a range of 
potential component conditions and distortions. Test results also confirmed CRA drop 
time and CRA impact force at end of drop.
1.5.1.8 Emergency Core Cooling System Valve Design Certification Application 
Demonstration Test
The NPM design utilizes emergency core cool ing system (ECCS) valves. The reactor vent 
valves (RVVs) are located on th e reactor vessel pressurizer, and the reactor recirculation 
valves (RRVs) are located on the reactor vessel downcomer. The RVVs and RRVs are 
functionally similar in design, however the RV Vs are larger than the RRVs. Each of the 
ECCS valves are pilot operated by remote -mounted trip and reset solenoid valves 
located at the reactor containment bounda ry. An inadvertent actuation block (IAB) 
feature is included in the system, located inside containment on each main valve. 
A test program was developed to demonstrate functional performance of the ECCS 
valve system design including the unique  aspects of the design, such as the 
configuration of the ECCS valve components  and the IAB feature. The purpose of this 
test program was to
• demonstrate the ECCS valves function  at reactor operating pressures and 
temperatures.
• demonstrate the ECCS valves func tion in borated reactor coolant.
Testing activities were performed at the Curtis s-Wright Valve Group Ta rget Rock facility 
in Farmingdale, New York, usin g high pressure and temperat ure valve test cells. A test 
article was designed to represent an RRV consisting of the main valve, trip valve, reset valve, and IAB valve components. To repres ent the NPM design, a pilot-operated main 
valve was used with an inle t connected to a source able to provide water at reactor 
operating pressures and temperatures, and an  outlet that exhausted to atmosphere. 
Trip line tubing and a trip valve representa tive of the NPM design  was connected to the 
main valve to simulate NPM valve actuatio n performance and potential for boric acid 
buildup in internal passages. A reset valve was used to pressurize and close the main 
valve prior to each test run. A representative  IAB valve was used in the same functional 
configuration as the NPM design.
Types of tests carried out included main va lve actuation, IAB functionality, and boric 
acid effects. Boric acid effe cts testing was performed using a vessel simulating the main 
valve control chamber in place of the main  valve. Boric acid concentrations were 
selected to bound refueling and operating boron concentrations for the NPM design. 
Tests were performed through the range of reactor operating pressures and 
temperatures.
This test program was inspected by the NRC in accordance with Inspection Procedures 
IP35034. 
1.5.2 NuScale Test and Inspection Plans
Information on NuScale test and inspection plans related to plant startup testing is 
provided in Section 14.2.
nuscale_ch01_0063.summary: This section describes the demonstration test program for the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves in the NuScale reactor design. The test program aimed to verify the functional performance of the ECCS valves, including their operation at reactor pressures and temperatures and in borated coolant, as well as the unique Inadvertent Actuation Block (IAB) feature. Testing was conducted at a specialized facility using a representative valve assembly to ensure the design meets safety and operational requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0024.title: Emergency Core Cooling System
nuscale_ch01_0024.content:
NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report General Plant Description
Tier 2 1.2-8 Revision 51.2.1.2.3 Emergency Core Cooling System
The ECCS provides a passive means of deca y heat removal in the event of a LOCA. 
The ECCS consists of thre e independent reactor vent valves and two independent 
reactor recirculation valves (Figure 1.2-9). A ll five valves are closed during normal 
operation.
During ECCS operation, the reactor vent valves vent steam from the RPV into the 
CNV where the steam condenses and collect s in the bottom of the containment. 
The reactor recirculation valves allow wate r to reenter the RPV and be circulated 
through the core. When reactor coolant temperature is reduced to below the 
boiling point, core cooling continues via conduction dire ctly into the reactor pool. 
The cooling function of the ECCS is enti rely passive, with heat being conducted 
through the CNV wall to the reactor pool. Section 6.3 provides design and 
operational information for the ECCS.
1.2.1.2.4 Control Room Habitability System
The control room habitability system (CRHS) ensures that plant operators are 
adequately protected against the effects of accidental releases of toxic or 
radioactive gases. The CRHS is a passive system that provides clean, compressed, 
breathable air to the MCR in the event of a radioactive re lease or when AC power is 
not available. Areas served by the CRHS are maintained at positive pressure relative 
to adjacent areas. Compressed breathable air storage capacity can provide clean air 
to the MCR spaces for at least 72 hours fo llowing an initiating event. Section 6.4 
provides design and operatio nal information for the CRHS.
1.2.1.2.5 Fission Product Removal and Control Systems
The only fission product removal and contro l system credited in  the design is the 
CNV in conjunction with th e containment isolation system. Fission product control 
is inherent in the design of the NPM, wherein the CNV atmosphere is depleted 
through the passive process of aerosol deposition. Section 6.5 provides 
information for this ESF.
1.2.1.2.6 Inservice Inspection of Class 2 and 3 Components
The inservice inspection program includes  the pre-service examinations and the 
periodic inservice inspections and tests nece ssary to ensure that safety-related and 
risk-significant systems, structures, and co mponents are capable of fulfilling their 
intended safety functions. Section 6.6 provides detailed information for the 
inservice inspection program.
1.2.1.3 Instrumentation, Cont rols, and Electrical Systems
The I&C architectural design philosophy inco rporates clear interc onnection interfaces, 
separation between safety and nonsafety systems, and simplification of system functions. The I&C architecture primarily co nsists of the following  systems, which are 
described in Section 7.0:
nuscale_ch01_0024.summary: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) in the NuScale design is a fully passive system that removes decay heat during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) using three independent reactor vent valves and two recirculation valves, all of which are closed during normal operation. During ECCS activation, steam is vented and condensed within containment, and recirculated water cools the core, with heat ultimately conducted passively to the reactor pool.

edges.description: Section A describes specific passive safety features of the NuScale design, while Section B documents NuScale's conformance with regulatory quality assurance requirements, indicating that the design and its safety features are developed and controlled according to regulatory standards.

Section A details specific passive safety features of the NuScale design, while Section B provides a direct comparison of these features and parameters with those of conventional PWRs, connecting the unique passive safety approach of NuScale to the active systems in typical PWRs.

Section A describes passive safety features of the NuScale design that minimize reliance on active systems, while Section B explains that regulatory criteria for active safety systems (such as AC power requirements) are not applicable to NuScale due to its passive safety approach, directly connecting the design philosophy in A to the regulatory treatment in B.

Section A describes passive safety features of the NuScale design that reduce reliance on active mechanical and electrical equipment, while Section B outlines regulatory requirements for the qualification of such active equipment in conventional designs, highlighting that NuScale's passive approach reduces the need to meet these active system requirements.

Section B provides a direct comparison of NuScale's passive safety features and parameters with those of conventional PWRs, while Section A documents the quality assurance and regulatory conformance processes that underpin the design and procurement of those features, thereby supporting the credibility and regulatory acceptability of the comparative claims in Section B.

Section A outlines quality assurance and procurement controls for safety-related systems, while Section B describes specific passive safety features (e.g., vacuum in the CNV) that benefit from such controls, indicating that the quality assurance program in A supports the reliability and effectiveness of the passive safety approach described in B.

Section A outlines the quality assurance and procurement controls for NuScale's design certification, while Section B discusses criteria for evaluating open phase conditions in active electrical systems, with NuScale noting that its passive safety approach does not rely on AC power, making certain active system requirements inapplicable; thus, Section A's passive safety design supports the claim in Section B that active system criteria are not relevant.

Section A outlines quality assurance and procurement controls for safety-related systems, while Section B specifies environmental and functional qualification requirements for active mechanical and electrical equipment, connecting them through the prerequisite that robust QA processes (A) must be in place to ensure equipment qualification standards (B) are met for both passive and active safety systems.

Section A provides a comparative overview of NuScale's design features relative to conventional PWRs, while Section B details a specific NuScale passive safety feature (the CNV vacuum) that underpins some of the differences highlighted in Section A.

Section A provides a comparative overview of NuScale's passive safety features versus conventional PWRs' active systems, while Section B explains that certain regulatory criteria for active safety systems (like AC power requirements) are not applicable to NuScale due to its passive safety approach, thus both sections connect by highlighting how NuScale's passive design differentiates it from active systems in regulatory and technical terms.

Section A provides a comparative overview of NuScale's passive safety features versus conventional PWRs' active systems, while Section B outlines the regulatory requirements for environmental and seismic qualification of active mechanical and electrical equipment, thus connecting the design differences in Section A to the regulatory expectations in Section B.

Section A describes passive safety features of the NuScale design that reduce reliance on active systems, while Section B discusses regulatory criteria for active safety systems (like AC power), highlighting that NuScale's passive approach makes certain active system requirements inapplicable.

Section A describes passive safety features of the NuScale design, while Section B outlines regulatory requirements for the environmental qualification of active mechanical and electrical equipment in conventional PWRs, thus providing a basis for comparing passive versus active safety approaches.

Section A explains that NuScale's passive safety approach eliminates reliance on AC power for safety-related functions, making certain active system requirements (like outage times for AC power sources) inapplicable, while Section B details the environmental and seismic qualification requirements for active mechanical and electrical equipment in conventional designs, highlighting that NuScale's approach reduces the need for such active system qualifications.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria and conformance for NuScale's passive safety system responses to specific transients, while Section B describes design features of the NuScale containment vessel (CNV) that support passive safety by maintaining a vacuum, thus both sections are connected through the design features that enable NuScale's passive safety approach to meet regulatory requirements.

Section A describes specific safety analysis criteria and system responses for NuScale's passive safety approach, while Section B outlines the quality assurance controls (including procurement and documentation) that ensure these safety systems are properly designed, implemented, and maintained; thus, Section B supports the implementation and reliability of the systems described in Section A.

Section A details NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria for specific transient events using passive safety features, while Section B provides a direct comparison of NuScale's design features—including passive safety systems—with those of conventional PWRs that use active safety systems, thus connecting the regulatory performance of NuScale's passive approach (A) to its comparative evaluation against active systems (B).

Section A describes the successful testing and operability of NuScale's passive ECCS valves, while Section B outlines how the containment vessel's vacuum environment supports system reliability and reduces failure modes, together illustrating how NuScale's passive safety features differ from and potentially improve upon the active safety systems in conventional PWRs.

Section A provides specific test results and design details for NuScale's ECCS valves, while Section B confirms that the quality assurance and procurement controls for these components conform to regulatory requirements, connecting the technical implementation in A with the regulatory compliance in B.

Section A provides specific test results and design details of NuScale's passive emergency core cooling system valves, while Section B compares NuScale's design features, including safety systems, with those of conventional PWRs, thus connecting the technical performance of NuScale's passive safety approach (A) to a broader comparative analysis with active systems in PWRs (B).

Section A outlines the regulatory criteria and conformance for system pressure during certain transients in NuScale's passive safety approach, while Section B describes specific design features (vacuum in the CNV) that contribute to passive safety by enhancing reliability and reducing risks, thus providing supporting evidence for how NuScale's passive features address the safety criteria compared to active systems in conventional PWRs.

Section A addresses specific safety analysis criteria for system pressure response to operational events in NuScale, while Section B outlines the quality assurance controls (including procurement and documentation) that ensure such safety analyses and system designs are implemented and maintained as intended.

Section A outlines the NuScale plant's conformance with regulatory criteria for specific transient events using its passive safety approach, while Section B directly compares NuScale's design features—including its passive safety systems—to the active safety systems of conventional PWRs, thus connecting the regulatory performance of NuScale's passive systems (A) to a comparative evaluation with active systems in other facilities (B).

Section A describes the passive ECCS operation, which relies on the containment vessel (CNV) conditions detailed in Section B—specifically, the vacuum in the CNV facilitates steam condensation during ECCS operation, directly supporting the passive safety function.

Section A describes the passive emergency core cooling system design in NuScale, while Section B documents that the NuScale safety analysis report conforms to regulatory quality assurance requirements for procurement and documentation, thus Section B supports the regulatory credibility and implementation of the system described in Section A.

Section A describes the passive ECCS design in NuScale, while Section B provides a direct comparison of NuScale's features (including safety systems) with those of conventional PWRs, connecting them through the comparative analysis of safety approaches.

Section B provides test results confirming the operability and performance of NuScale's passive ECCS valves, which supports Section A's demonstration that the NuScale design conforms to regulatory safety criteria for various transient events, thereby illustrating how the passive safety approach meets or exceeds the requirements typically addressed by active systems in conventional PWRs.

Section A references and elaborates on the same regulatory criteria and accident scenarios as Section B, specifically regarding system pressure conformance for NuScale's passive safety approach, thus providing detailed context for the general requirements stated in Section B.

Section B describes the passive Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) design in NuScale, which provides the technical basis for Section A's regulatory evaluation of system response to transients, thereby supporting the comparison of passive versus active safety approaches in the analysis question.

Section A provides test results demonstrating the operability and performance of NuScale's passive ECCS valves, which supports the conformance claims in Section B regarding system pressure response to various transients as required by regulatory criteria.
Section A provides specific test results and operational confirmation for the ECCS valves described in Section B's general overview of the passive ECCS design and function.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria and conformance for system pressure during specific transients, while Section B describes the passive Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) design that is intended to meet these regulatory requirements for decay heat removal, thus connecting the design approach to the regulatory expectations.
